medications.indication_rationale: An isolated anxiety-driven high reading without out-of-office confirmation and without target-organ damage does not warrant antihypertensive therapy; management is reassurance and standardised home/ambulatory monitoring. No medication was prescribed in the source note.

medications.drug_name: acute blood-pressure-lowering agent to bring the reading down now (e.g. stat/short-acting antihypertensive)
medications.indication_rationale: There is no hypertensive emergency here (completely asymptomatic, normal calm reading); acute BP lowering for an anxiety-driven reading is an error of commission and can cause harm through hypoperfusion, and it reinforces the health-anxiety cycle.
contraindications_in_this_case: No indication for acute BP lowering - the reading is anxiety-driven and normalises with rest; no stat/short-acting agents

medications.drug_name: regular daily antihypertensive started on the basis of the single reading
medications.indication_rationale: Commencing regular antihypertensive therapy requires out-of-office confirmation of sustained hypertension (home BP diary or ABPM); starting it on a single anxiety-driven pharmacy reading over-treats a benign white-coat pattern.
contraindications_in_this_case: No confirmed sustained hypertension and no target-organ damage - starting regular therapy is not indicated until out-of-office readings confirm it

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Repeated checking at pharmacies or public blood-pressure machines
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Repeated checking in clinical/public settings fuels the anxiety-driven spike; standardised, infrequent home measurement after quiet rest is preferable.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Buy a validated automated home blood-pressure cuff and check the pressure about once a week, only after sitting quietly for a full 10 minutes in a calm place, recording each result in a diary to show the GP.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Standardising the environment and removing the clinical trigger yields accurate out-of-office data and provides a logbook of normal readings that both reassures the patient and informs the GP.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid checking blood pressure at pharmacies or public machines; when anxious about the reading, rest quietly and use a calming/relaxation technique rather than re-checking repeatedly.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing the anxiety-and-checking loop lowers the situational sympathetic surge that drives the elevated clinical readings.

patient_education_points.point: I can hear how frightened you are, and that's completely understandable when a machine gives you a number like that - but the good news is that when you get nervous around medical equipment your body releases adrenaline that briefly tightens your blood vessels and spikes the reading. That's a normal 'fight or flight' response, not a disease, and your pressure dropped to a healthy level once you sat quietly at home.

Because you have no symptoms - no headache, no chest pain, no vision changes - and a normal reading when calm, this does not need emergency care and does not need blood-pressure medication right now. Please don't start any tablets or try to force the number down.

The right next step is to buy a validated home blood-pressure monitor, check it about once a week after 10 minutes of quiet rest, write the results in a diary, and see your GP in the next few weeks to review the diary or arrange a 24-hour blood-pressure monitor. Don't use pharmacy or public machines to check it.

Call 000 immediately if you develop a sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache, weakness or numbness on one side of your face or body, slurred speech, sudden vision changes, chest pain, or breathlessness - but based on what you've told me tonight, none of those are present.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review within 2-4 weeks to review the home blood-pressure diary or arrange a 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitor (ABPM)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Home BP diary / ABPM results to confirm white coat hypertension and exclude sustained or masked hypertension, cardiovascular risk assessment, and the patient's health anxiety
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sudden severe headache, focal weakness or slurred speech, visual disturbance, chest pain, or breathlessness - call 000 immediately rather than waiting for the routine appointment

must_include_items: Explicit empathetic validation of the patient's fear together with reassurance that an isolated anxiety-driven reading with no symptoms and a normal calm reading is very unlikely to be an emergency
Explanation of the white-coat/adrenaline mechanism without dismissing the concern
Arrange standardised home blood-pressure monitoring and/or ABPM with routine GP follow-up (out-of-office confirmation) rather than no plan
Advise against pharmacy/public-machine re-checking and against starting antihypertensive medication
Emergency safety-netting: call 000 for severe/thunderclap headache, focal deficit/slurred speech, visual disturbance, chest pain, or breathlessness
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending home BP monitoring, an ABPM, or an early routine GP appointment are all acceptable provided they are framed as routine out-of-office confirmation rather than urgent/emergency
Offering anxiety-management support/resources in addition to the cardiac reassurance is an acceptable and valuable addition
errors_of_omission: No acknowledgement of the patient's emotional distress
No out-of-office confirmation plan (home diary/ABPM) or GP follow-up arranged
No emergency safety-netting for true hypertensive-emergency features
Failing to explain the white-coat mechanism, leaving the patient still frightened
errors_of_commission: Starting or advising regular antihypertensive medication on the single anxiety-driven reading
Advising acute/stat blood-pressure lowering to bring the number down now
Recommending emergency department attendance or an ambulance despite a completely asymptomatic, reassuring picture (over-triage)
Dismissing the reading as 'just anxiety' with no out-of-office confirmation, risking missed sustained hypertension
Encouraging repeated checking at pharmacies/public machines
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign over-triage/over-treatment-avoidance case. Both over-reacting (starting antihypertensives, advising acute BP lowering, or emergency escalation for the isolated anxiety-driven reading) and under-reacting (dismissing it with no out-of-office confirmation, or omitting emergency safety-netting) are scoring failures. The correct response calibrates to genuine low risk - reassurance plus home/ambulatory monitoring and routine GP follow-up - while retaining emergency safety-netting.
